articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Dec. 21, 1912. On Philadelphia's 'unconventional' characters: John Cromwell, who opened music sight-reading classes over ninety years earlier; the Clarkson brothers, school teachers and accountants who managed Black Philadelphians' business affairs; pioneer caterer Robert Bogle and bandmaster Frank Johnson, who wore shad-belly coats and knee breeches far into the 1840s; the altruist James McC. Crummill; the bombastic orator White (given name garbled in OCR), active 1840-1856; debater John L. Johnson, whose unpublished manuscript on 'The Egyptian and African races' his widow offered to the Banneker Institute; Ebenezer Black, secretary of beneficial societies; and Belfast Burton, druggist's aide called 'Doctor' and Bishop Allen's most important A.M.E. layman. Dr. J. J. G. Bias, cupper and bleeder on Sixth below Lombard and local A.M.E. preacher, led the 'Bias mob' defense against pro-slavery firemen and thugs c. 1842.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 26, 1912. Retrospective on the Eighth (1848) General Conference of the A.M.E. Church: Daniel A. Payne won his fight for an educated ministry (a four-year graded course of studies made 'positive law') and started a church-history movement; the Presiding Elder system originated here; Rev. A. R. Green was elected Book Steward and the New England Conference instituted. Bolivar names Alexander W. Wayman, Stephen Smith (the church's financial mainstay and benefactor of the Home for Aged and Infirm Colored Persons), George Hogarth and David Ware (who funded Payne's church magazine), lay leader Dr. Bias (whose sympathy resolution Payne seconded), John M. Brown, and school teacher Adam Driver. A strong minority including Payne, Willis Nazrey, A. R. Green, A. W. Wayman and N. C. W. Cannon opposed licensing women to preach; it was the first conference after Bishop Waters' death, recounted in Bishop Quinn's Episcopal Address.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 1, 1913. History of Black catering in Philadelphia: colored men served the pre-Revolutionary 'Assembly' balls; ice cream was originated by a colored man whose shop stood behind the Philadelphia Savings Fund (his daughter later made it at what is now 1014 Walnut St., sold at a dollar a quart); restaurant men Jacob Nixon, James LeCount, James Prosser, Cato Collins and Jeremiah Bowser supplied entertainments; the Augustines began in 1816; St. John Appo was a confectioner on Sixth near Spruce, with the Bartholomew brothers and Joseph Rodgers underselling on ice cream. Robert Bogle originated contract catering and served as funeral director, dictating mourning dress; he lived on Eighth near Sansom and later 10th and Pine, was immortalized in Nicholas Biddle's 'Ode to Bogle,' raised three daughters who became school teachers, and at his death in 1837 was buried from St. Thomas' Church in the old Fifth Street cemetery (now Eden Cemetery). Early assistants included Sheppard, Johnson and Chew, later Emanuel Morris, David W. Brown and Philip A. Holmes Sr.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, May 3, 1913. On Black cricket and baseball: after 'town ball' came three colored cricket clubs — Metamora, Olive and Diligent — playing behind the Academy of Music and at 16th-17th and Pine; standout cricketers included James H. Francis, Francis Wood, William H. Mills, William Swan, James P. Clay, Jacob R. Ballard, Henry Jones Jr., Frank J. R. Jones, James H. Roberts, Edward Evans and Daniel Seymour. The Excelsiors formed in spring 1866, playing at Diamond Cottage Park, Camden (players Frank J. R. Jones, Jacob Price, George W. A. Scott, James H. Francis, Thomas Irons, Frank Wood, Andrew Glasgow, Archie Sparrow); the Pythian Club, promoted and captained by Octavius V. Catto with quarters in Liberty Hall, absorbed Excelsior stars including Pliny I. Locke and played until 1872 against Washington, Harrisburg, Chicago and Albany clubs. Survivors named include Joshua Atkins, Andrew J. Jones, Raymond J. Burr, Spencer Handley and Henry Boyer Jr.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Nov. 29, 1913. Biography of Absalom Jones (continued from previous issue): born enslaved in Sussex, Delaware, Nov. 6, 1746; self-taught from a primer; came to Philadelphia in 1762, married in 1770, bought real estate and taught a day school. A founder and officer of the Free African Society; served the city in the 1793 yellow fever plague (noted by Benjamin Rush and Mathew Carey); ordained deacon by Bishop White in 1795 and priest in 1804 (St. Thomas' cornerstone laid Feb. 1792, worship begun July 1794); helped found and was president of an insurance company in 1806; wrote a national protest against colonization; with Richard Allen and James Forten raised a battalion of colored soldiers for the War of 1812, publicly thanked by Mayor Clarkson in Independence Square; laid hands on Richard Allen at his 1816 consecration as A.M.E. bishop; officer of the first secular convention against colonization in 1817; friend of Prince Hall and himself a Mason. He died Feb. 13, 1818.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 24, 1914. Essay on organization and unity from 1787 forward: the Free African Society's founding, echoed by Newport and Charleston's Brown Fellowship Society; Absalom Jones dragged from his knees at St. George's; the insurance company Jones formed when colored risks were refused; the first secular convention (Philadelphia, 1817) against deportation; Benjamin Rush taking a pew at St. Thomas'. Platform defenders named include Frederick Hinton, John C. Bowers, Samuel R. Ward, Charles L. Remond, Robert Purvis and Isaiah C. Wear; the West Indian Emancipation celebration under Dr. James G. G. Bias was defended by fighting against Irish and hoodlum firemen; the Equal Rights League was worked out by William D. Forten, Jacob C. White Jr., Octavius V. Catto, William Nesbitt and John W. Simpson. Bolivar closes urging support of the new Advancement Society (NAACP).

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 6, 1912. Occasioned by the M.E. Annual Conference at old St. George's Church (Fourth and New Sts.), where Absalom Jones and Richard Allen worshipped, were relegated to the gallery, and were finally forced out — Jones dragged from his knees at prayer — the 'blessing in disguise' that produced St. Thomas' and Bethel. Also a tour of the South Street theatre district: Dr. Bias's paraders took refuge behind the old theatre in the riots of c. 1842, and Benjamin Welsh's banner (now in the Adger Collection at the Old Folks Home) was hidden in a cellar nearby; neighbors included Littleton Hubert, the Curtis family, centenarian David W. Brown, Rev. Joshua P. B. Eddy, James Forten and Joseph Cassey (Lombard St.), and 'blind Alice' (d. 1802, aged 116). At the Walnut Street Theatre (opened 1808), Rachel Lloyd — whose home was an Underground Railroad station and who died aged 104 — kept the restaurant, followed by John Nixon and Mrs. Dickerson; in 1826 the Kemble engagement orchestra included four colored musicians, Joseph and William Appo and the Newton brothers, with slaveholder Pierce Butler playing flute to court Fanny Kemble.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Dec. 6, 1913. On women as public speakers: pioneers Lucretia Mott, Abby Kimber and Fanny Wright, followed by Abby Kelley Foster, Lucy Stone, Angelina and Sarah Grimké, Mary Grew, Graceanna Lewis and Hester Reckless (a colored woman of Philadelphia), then Sojourner Truth, Harriet Tubman, Frances E. W. Harper, Anna E. Dickinson, Fanny M. Jackson Coppin and Mary Church Terrell. The main subject is preacher Jarena Lee: born free at Cape May, N.J., Feb. 11, 1783; schoolteacher and Sabbath-school founder; her self-written journal was published in Philadelphia; she came into A.M.E. communion after dramatically finishing a floundering preacher's sermon, was elevated to the ministry by Bishop Richard Allen, often filled Bethel and other pulpits, and did yeoman service through several states.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 2, 1913. Conclusion of the Sarah Ash story: to save a runaway slave girl from being remanded, Ash sent the girl's first protector out of the city to Trenton, then took the stand and swore the girl's parents were white foreigners who died leaving her an orphan — the girl was cleared, adopted by Mr. and Mrs. John Gilmore, and grew up to marry one of Philadelphia's worthiest men; her grandson became a Presbyterian minister in New York State. Also transcribes an old programme of a June 3, 1875 Sinking Fund entertainment at the First (African) Presbyterian Church, 7th below Bainbridge: C. Samuel Adger musical director, with Mrs. Lucy A. Adger, Caroline R. LeCount, Edith Webb, Mary E. Hipple, Gertrude Bustill, Thomas J. Bowers, Chris J. Perry, James L. Jenkins, Henry Boyer and Rev. R. B. Johns; tickets from Susan Sylva, E. Bustill, Abigail White, Robert Adger and Benjamin G. Hill.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 20, 1912. Review of the new book 'The Pennsylvania Negro' crediting the Quakers' practical aid: Anthony Benezet's first school for Black Philadelphians (1750); gradual-emancipation work beginning 1688 in Pennsylvania; free-labor stores at Fifth and Cherry; a Quaker-founded school of 1802 with a colored woman teacher; Richard Humphreys' money starting the Institute for Colored Youth; the shelter for colored children and the House of Industry; a Quaker first recognizing and funding Benjamin Banneker's 1792 almanac. Bolivar names Benjamin Franklin, Benjamin Rush and Benjamin Lay as converts to bold antislavery action; the Garretts and Poseys of Delaware; Chester County's Underground Railroad (Smedley's history); and 1831 movement Quakers Lucretia Mott, Abby Kimber, Dinah Mendenhall, James Mott, J. G. Whittier and Thomas Wharton.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 18, 1914. On Philadelphia as the center of early Black publishing: courageous printers included the Merrihew brothers, King & Baird, Duross, and the colored printer James McC. Crummill (Vine near Fifth, over seventy years earlier). Philadelphia presses issued Benjamin Banneker's almanacs (1792-95), Rev. Absalom Jones's 1804 protest against deportation, Russell Parrott's 1814 argument, the 1817 anti-colonization convention proceedings, Prince Saunders (1818), Bishop Allen's autobiography (1831), the anti-slavery convention reports of 1831-36, the first colored magazine under William Whipper, the Demosthenian Shield (1841), Dr. Joseph Willson's anonymous 'Sketches of Colored Society in Philadelphia' (1841), Martin R. Delany's 'Condition, Elevation and Emigration' (1852), Rev. William Douglass's Sermons and Annals, Frances E. W. Harper's first books (1855), Lorenzo Blackson's allegory, Rev. Jeremiah Asher's autobiography (1862), Rev. William T. Catto's First Presbyterian history (1857), William Still's 'The Underground Railroad,' Jarena Lee's autobiography (1847), and later Du Bois and R. R. Wright Jr. The A.M.E. Book Concern on Pine Street issued more books than any other colored publishing house.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 28, 1914. Biography of Russell Parrott (d. Aug. 30, 1824, aged 33), the young orator close to Absalom Jones, Bishop Allen, John Gloucester and James Forten from 1810 on: denied schooling because of color yet a polished writer, he conceived the benevolent society first known as the Friendly Sons of St. Thomas (afterwards Sons of St. Thomas, reorganized 1823 and 1857 and still alive in 1914). His printed address of January 1, 1814 on the abolition of the slave trade — with Rev. Absalom Jones giving the invocation — is the oldest known printed secular address by a Black American, surpassing even Prince Saunders; taken up as a candidate for Holy Orders by the St. Thomas vestry on Jan. 28, 1822 and licensed as lay reader by Bishop White, he died while studying for the priesthood. The Sons and Daughters of St. Thomas and the Female Baptist Assistance Society erected his grave monument, and his remains now lie in Eden Cemetery.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 25, 1913. Review of Mary White Ovington's sociological study 'Half a Man,' compared with Ray Stannard Baker's work and with race fiction pro and con — Uncle Tom's Cabin, Martineau's 'The Hour and the Man' on Toussaint L'Ouverture, Tourgée's 'Pactolus Prime,' Anna Dickinson's 'What Answer,' Griggs's 'The Hindered Hand,' Du Bois's 'Quest of the Silver Fleece,' Dunbar's 'The Sport of the Gods,' and the hostile 'Clansman.' Bolivar places Ovington in a Philadelphia statistical lineage begun by Caleb Needles in 1838 (the census pamphlet of Black Philadelphia), continued by Joseph Truman in 1847, Du Bois's 'The Philadelphia Negro,' and Richard R. Wright Jr.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 18, 1913. Prompted by the new 'Booklovers' society, a history of antebellum Black literary societies: young men studied the U.S. Constitution despite Dred Scott — foremost Jacob C. White Jr., Octavius V. Catto, George E. Stevens, Isaiah C. Wears, Parker T. Smith and William H. Minton — with women participants Sarah M. Douglass, Grace A. Mapps, Caroline E. Harding, Mary Shadd (afterward Cary), Charlotte L. Forten and Frances Ellen Watkins (Harper). Meetings and debates were held in a small hall on Walnut above Sixth (the Curtis Building site) and at Franklin Hall, Sixth below Arch, where Wears bested pro-slavery champions; other participants were Rev. Jabez P. Campbell, Rev. William T. Catto, Nathaniel Durham, Joseph C. Bustill, David Bowser and actor S. Morgan Smith — successors of Frederick A. Hinton, William Whipper, James McC. Crummill, Junius C. Morrell and John C. Bowers. Bolivar lists the season-of-1850 papers (Vance, Gilmore, Turner, Smith, Brister, Fosset, Taylor, Gibbs, Green, Weaver, Catto, Shadd, Campbell, Asher, Dorsey and others) and closes by adding early Tribune workers unintentionally omitted from a prior list.

events.name: Eighth General Conference of the A.M.E. Church
events.description: The 1848 General Conference: Daniel A. Payne's educated-ministry course adopted as positive law, Presiding Elder system introduced, New England Conference instituted, A. R. Green elected Book Steward, action against divorce and christening/burial fees, minority fight against licensing women to preach; first conference after Bishop Waters' death (recalled by W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 26, 1912).

events.name: Formation of the Equal Rights League
events.description: Civil-rights organization 'formed and worked out' by young Philadelphia activists; Bolivar credits it with the first real Civil Rights results (Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 24, 1914).

events.name: Expulsion of Absalom Jones and Richard Allen from St. George's Methodist Church
events.location: St. George's Methodist Church, Fourth and New Streets, Philadelphia
events.description: Absalom Jones was assaulted while at prayer, dragged from his knees and thrown out of St. George's; the episode produced 'a general cohesiveness of the colored people' and the founding of St. Thomas' under Jones and Bethel under Allen (recalled by W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 6, 1912).

events.name: Mixed orchestra for the Kemble engagement, Walnut Street Theatre
events.description: When Charles Kemble and his daughter Fanny came to America, the Walnut Street Theatre orchestra was mixed, four members being colored: Joseph and William Appo and the Newton brothers; slaveholder Pierce Butler took a flute seat to be near Fanny Kemble (Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 6, 1912).

events.name: Court rescue of a fugitive slave girl by Sarah Ash's testimony
events.description: Sarah Ash sent the girl's first protector to Trenton to keep her from testifying, then swore in court that the runaway girl's parents were white foreigners who died friendless; the girl was cleared and adopted by Mr. and Mrs. John Gilmore (Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 2, 1913).

events.name: Sinking Fund entertainment, First African Presbyterian Church
events.location: First African Presbyterian Church, Seventh below Bainbridge, Philadelphia
events.description: Entertainment by the ladies of the Sinking Fund for the benefit of the church; programme transcribed by Bolivar from an original (Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 2, 1913).

events.name: Russell Parrott's oration on the abolition of the slave trade
events.description: Printed address of January 1, 1814 celebrating the abolition of the slave trade — the oldest printed secular address by a Black American known to collectors; Rev. Absalom Jones made the invocation and a choir sang hymns and anthems (Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 28, 1914).

events.name: Founding of the Excelsior and Pythian base ball clubs
events.location: Philadelphia and Diamond Cottage Park, Camden
events.description: The Excelsiors formed in spring 1866 playing at Camden; the Pythian Club, promoted and captained by Octavius V. Catto with quarters in Liberty Hall, followed and played until 1872 (Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, May 3, 1913).
